competitor_patterns: Top-ranking pages (Outdoorgearlab, Bikerumor) have extremely high word counts — 16,000–25,000 words — built around actual multi-product testing frameworks with named testers and testing dates, giving them E-E-A-T authority our 3,500–3,900 word posts cannot match without a review structure

All top-3 competitors use product-specific H2s (e.g. 'Julbo Density', 'Oakley Sutro Lite') alongside category H2s, signalling to Google that the page covers individual products in depth, not just abstract criteria

Every top-3 page includes both FAQ schema and a comparison table — our 'Best Cycling Glasses 2026' post has a table but our Oakley Alternatives post lacks one, and the table+FAQ combo appears non-negotiable for featured snippet eligibility

Competitors publish and visibly date-update their posts (Jan 2026, May 2026) multiple times per year, signalling freshness — their titles often include the exact year ('2026') and the word 'Tested', both of which drive CTR and freshness signals simultaneously

Top-ranking competitor meta descriptions are benefit-led and quantified ('We tested 18 of the best', 'dozens of top sunglasses from Smith, Roka, 100%…') — they promise a specific deliverable (tested, ranked, compared), not a general topic
our_gaps: Our posts lack third-party product comparisons with named competing products in dedicated H2s — we reference alternatives generically but never give competitors named H2 sections the way Bikerumor does, which limits our topical coverage signals

Word count is 3,500–3,900 vs competitor range of 6,600–24,900 — even the shortest top-3 page (Cyclist at 6,632 words) is nearly double ours, meaning we lack the content depth Google rewards for high-competition head terms

Our 'Oakley Alternatives' post (our highest-impression comparison angle with 316 combined impressions across 3 Oakley queries) has no comparison table — this is a direct structural gap vs all top-ranking competitors and hurts featured snippet eligibility

We have no content targeting budget/value cycling glasses sub-niches (Outdoorgearlab ranks for 'Best Value Cycling Sunglasses' and 'Best Performance Sunglasses on a Tight Budget') — a Velluto price-vs-performance angle is completely absent

None of our posts include a visible testing methodology or 'Why trust us' section — Cyclist explicitly uses 'How do we test and why trust Cyclist's advice?' as an H2, which is a direct E-E-A-T signal Google's quality raters look for
keyword_opportunities: best cycling sunglasses under 100 — budget-angle sub-category that Outdoorgearlab and 220triathlon rank for; Velluto's Italian design at accessible price point is a natural fit
lightweight cycling glasses — directly maps to Velluto's 25g frame USP; not covered in any current post and differentiates from Oakley, which competitors benchmark against
cycling glasses interchangeable lenses — product-feature query with commercial intent; our VellutoPuro/VellutoVisione click-in system is a direct answer but no post targets this phrase
anti fog cycling glasses — functional safety query with high purchase intent; our built-in anti-fog system is a core differentiator not yet used as a primary post angle

oakley radar alternative 2026 — 48 impressions at position 4.0, the closest we are to page 1 for any Oakley query; a dedicated post targeting 'Oakley Radar' specifically (vs our generic alternatives post) would capture this with minimal competition
next_post_guidelines: Target 'oakley radar alternative 2026' as the primary keyword for tomorrow's post — it sits at position 4.0 with 48 impressions and 2.1% CTR, making it the single nearest-to-page-1 Oakley query; write a dedicated 'Oakley Radar vs Velluto StradaPro' comparison post, not a generic alternatives roundup

Add a product comparison table to every future post — the 'Oakley Alternatives' post (201 impressions, 3.0% CTR) has no table; all top-3 competitors use tables, and Google's featured snippet for comparison queries almost always pulls from structured table data

Structure the next post with named competitor product H2s (e.g. '## Oakley Radar Eye vs Velluto StradaPro', '## Tifosi Rail vs Velluto StradaPro') so Google indexes the page for multiple product-pair queries simultaneously, not just the head term

Include a visible 'How We Evaluated These Glasses' section of at least 200 words covering real-ride conditions, lens systems tested, and fit criteria — this directly addresses the E-E-A-T gap vs Cyclist and Bikerumor and should appear before the first product comparison

Write meta descriptions as a quantified promise: include a number ('7 alternatives tested'), a named competitor ('vs Oakley Radar'), and a result-oriented phrase ('find out which wins for road cyclists under €150') — mirror the formula used by Outdoorgearlab and Bikerumor that drives their higher CTRs

Increase target word count to minimum 5,500 words for any post competing on head terms ('best cycling glasses', 'best cycling sunglasses') — fill the gap with per-product deep dives, a buying criteria section, a lens guide, and real-ride scenarios, not padding

Add a '30-day trial' and 'free shipping over €99' callout box within the post body (not just in nav/footer) — this is a conversion and trust differentiator that no competitor can claim and should appear near the first Velluto mention and again in the conclusion
seo_quick_wins: Add a comparison table to the existing 'Best Oakley Alternatives for Cyclists 2026' post immediately — it has 201 impressions at position 6.8 and is the highest-impression post without a table; a table with columns (Weight, Lens System, Trial Policy, Price) gives Google a featured snippet extraction target and can move it from pos 6.8 to top 5 within weeks

Fix the 'veluto' (misspelling) brand query — 257 impressions at 0.4% CTR and position 5.4 means users searching for Velluto with a typo are not finding us; add a homepage or FAQ entry that explicitly addresses 'Looking for Velluto? You've found us — spelled V-E-L-L-U-T-O' to capture this branded misspell traffic

Append the current year ('2026') and the word 'Tested' or 'Reviewed' to every post meta title that targets a 'best X' query — our 'Best Cycling Glasses 2026' post already does this and achieves 3.3% CTR (top performing), while posts missing the year pattern underperform; standardise this across all buying guide titles
meta_title_fixes: veluto → New title: Velluto Cycling Glasses — Official Store | velluto-shop.com | Why: The misspelling query (257 impressions, 0.4% CTR) is almost certainly brand navigation intent; a title that corrects the spelling, signals 'official', and includes the domain builds instant recognition and lifts CTR from 0.4% toward the 5%+ we see for the correctly-spelled 'velluto' brand query

oakley alternatives → New title: Best Oakley Alternatives 2026 — Tested for Cyclists | Why: Current position 6.8 with 3.0% CTR; adding '2026', 'Tested', and 'for Cyclists' targets the same modifiers that drive clicks on competitor titles (Bikerumor, Outdoorgearlab) and differentiates from generic sunglasses alternatives pages

oakley sunglasses alternative → New title: Oakley Sunglasses Alternative? Try These 7 Picks | Why: Position 6.9, 1.5% CTR; a curiosity/challenge format ('Try These 7') with a specific number outperforms generic 'Best X' for mid-funnel comparison queries and fits within 60 chars

best cycling sunglasses → New title: Best Cycling Sunglasses 2026 — Tested & Ranked | Why: Position 7.9, 2.0% CTR; mirrors exact title formula of the #1 ranking Outdoorgearlab page ('Tested & Ranked') which signals the same content type and earns higher CTR from users who trust review-style content

oakley radar alternative → New title: Oakley Radar Alternative 2026 — Lighter & Cheaper? | Why: Position 4.0, 2.1% CTR — this is our nearest-to-top-3 Oakley query; a title that poses a direct value question ('Lighter & Cheaper?') triggers curiosity clicks and pre-answers Velluto's 25g weight USP before the user even lands on the page
content_quick_wins: best cycling sunglasses 2026 (pos 8.5) → Topic: Expand 'Best Cycling Glasses 2026' post to 6,000+ words with named per-product H2s | Add: Individual H2 sections for each compared product (e.g. '## Oakley Sutro Lite', '## Tifosi Rail') with a mini review paragraph each, a 7-column comparison table (Weight / Lens Type / Anti-Fog / Trial / Price / UV / Fit), and a 'Who should buy each' summary — this directly replicates the structure of the pos-1 Outdoorgearlab page that ranks for this query

oakley alternatives (pos 6.8) → Topic: Rebuild 'Best Oakley Alternatives for Cyclists 2026' post with a structured comparison table | Add: A side-by-side HTML table with Oakley Radar / Oakley Sutro Lite / Velluto StradaPro / Tifosi Rail / 100% Speedcraft across Weight, Lens System, Anti-Fog, Return Policy, Price — the absence of a table is the single biggest structural gap for this query and is the fastest lever to move from pos 6.8 to top 5

best cycling glasses 2026 (pos 7.3) → Topic: Create a dedicated 'Best Road Cycling Glasses 2026' content cluster landing page that consolidates the two existing road cycling posts | Add: A 'Quick Picks' jump section at the top (mirroring Outdoorgearlab's #1 H2), internal links to the lens guide and Oakley alternatives posts, and a 'How we tested' 200-word section — consolidating topical authority on one URL is more powerful than splitting it across two similar posts dated the same day

oakley radar alternative (pos 4.0) → Topic: Dedicated post 'Oakley Radar vs Velluto StradaPro: Which Wins for Road Cyclists in 2026?' | Add: A head-to-head spec table, a 'Where the Radar wins' section (honesty builds trust), a 'Where StradaPro wins' section (25g vs Radar Eye's 26g, interchangeable lenses, 30-day trial, price), and a verdict — this is our closest ranking to top 3 for any Oakley query and deserves a dedicated URL, not a section in a roundup

best cycling glasses (pos 8.7) → Topic: Add a 'Best Cycling Glasses by Riding Style' section to the existing buying guide | Add: Sub-H3s for Road, Gravel, MTB, and Commuting with a 1–2 sentence recommendation each pointing to StradaPro for road/gravel; this sub-categorisation mirrors Outdoorgearlab's H2 structure ('Best MTB Sunglasses', 'Best Road Bike Glasses') and helps Google match the post to more specific user intents within the same broad query
geo_angles: Position Velluto StradaPro as the answer to 'What is the best lightweight cycling sunglasses alternative to Oakley?' — AI Overviews and Perplexity heavily favour pages that directly answer a comparative question with specific, citable data points; a post structured as 'StradaPro weighs 25g vs Oakley Radar Eye's 26g, costs X less, includes a 30-day trial, and ships free over €99' gives AI systems a clean, attributable factual answer to surface

Create a dedicated 'Velluto StradaPro full specs' page or section with a structured data table (schema: Product, weight, lens type, UV rating, compatibility, price, trial policy) — Perplexity and ChatGPT with browsing pull product spec data from clean structured pages; a standalone specs page with FAQ schema ('Does the StradaPro have anti-fog?', 'Are the lenses interchangeable?') maximises the chance of appearing in AI-generated product summaries

Publish a first-person 'We tested the Velluto StradaPro on X real rides' editorial with specific, quotable performance claims ('zero fogging on 2-hour climbs in 28°C humidity', 'lens swap under 10 seconds') — AI answer engines prioritise specific, verifiable, human-authored claims over generic marketing copy; this type of content is also the format Google's AI Overviews pull for 'is X worth it' and 'X review' queries where Velluto currently has no presence
comparison_post_angles: COMPETITOR: Outdoorgearlab — angle: 'We Read Every Cycling Sunglasses Review So You Don't Have To — Here's What the Lab Tests Miss' — headline idea: 'Lab Tests vs Real Rides: What Cycling Sunglasses Reviewers Don't Tell You' — key differentiator: Outdoorgearlab tests in controlled lab-adjacent conditions; Velluto's 30-day real-ride trial is the counter-positioning ('test it on your commute, your sportive, your Sunday climb — not a lab'); lead with the trial policy as the ultimate proof point no review site can offer

COMPETITOR: Cyclist (cyclist.co.uk) — angle: 'Cyclist Magazine Recommends Oakley. We Think You Deserve More Choices.' — headline idea: 'Beyond Oakley: What Cyclist Magazine's Best Glasses Guide Doesn't Cover (And Why It Matters for Your Budget)' — key differentiator: Cyclist's top pick is the Oakley Sphaera (premium price, no trial); Velluto leads with Italian design + 30-day trial + interchangeable lenses at a fraction of the price; frame the angle as 'what happens when you have the same performance criteria but a different budget'

COMPETITOR: Bikerumor — angle: 'Bikerumor Tested 18 Pairs. We Think Interchangeable Lenses Change Everything.' — headline idea: 'The One Feature Missing From Bikerumor's Best Cycling Sunglasses List — And Why It Matters Most for Road Cyclists' — key differentiator: Bikerumor's top picks (Julbo Density, Oakley Sutro Lite, POC Devour) are single-lens or photochromic; Velluto's VellutoPuro + VellutoVisione click-in system addresses the fundamental problem none of those solve — changing light conditions mid-ride without carrying a second pair; lead with the lens system as the product angle, not the brand
